title: The Negroן¿½s Complaint.
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Forced from home and all its pleasures,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Africן¿½s coast I left forlorn;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To increase a strangerן¿½s treasures,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½er the raging billows borne.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Men from England bought and sold me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Paid my price in paltry gold;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, though slave they have enrollן¿½d me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Minds are never to be sold.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still in thought as free as ever,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What are Englandן¿½s rights, I ask,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Me from my delights to sever,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Me to torture, me to task?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fleecy locks and black complexion
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cannot forfeit natureן¿½s claim;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Skins may differ, but affection
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dwells in white and black the same.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why did all-creating Nature
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Make the plant for which we toil?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sighs must fan it, tears must water,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sweat of ours must dress the soil.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Think, ye masters iron-hearted,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lolling at your jovial boards,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Think how many backs have smarted
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the sweets your cane affords.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is there, as ye sometimes tells us,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is there One who reigns on high?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Has he bid you buy and sell us,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Speaking from his throne, the sky?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ask him, if your knotted scourges,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Matches, blood-extorting screws,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are the means that duty urges
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Agents of his will to use?


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hark! he answersן¿½wild tornadoes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Strewing yonder sea with wrecks;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wasting towns, plantations, meadows,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are the voice with which he speaks.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He, foreseeing what vexations
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Africן¿½s sons should undergo,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fixן¿½d their tyrantsן¿½ habitations
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where his whirlwinds answerן¿½no.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By our blood in Afric wasted,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere our necks received the chain;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the miseries that we tasted,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Crossing in your barks the main;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By our sufferings, since ye brought us
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the man-degrading mart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All sustainן¿½d by patience, taught us
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Only by a broken heart;


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Deem our nation brutes no longer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till some reason ye shall find
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Worthier of regard, and stronger
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than the colour of our kind.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Slaves of gold, whose sordid dealings
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tarnish all your boasted powers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Prove that you have human feelings,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere you proudly question ours!
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, William Cowper delivers a powerful performance in "The Negroן¿½s Complaint."...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
